topic: Someone receives an apology letter years after the incident
raw_output:
[story 1]

The envelope, thick and cream-colored, sat on the cluttered desk like a small, damning artifact. Liam picked it up, his knuckles white against the heavy paper. It was dated nearly seven years ago, the ink slightly faded, but the handwriting was undeniably hers. He didn't need to open it to know the contents would ignite a slow, simmering rage. He crumpled the letter once, twice, the sound sharp in the quiet apartment. His jaw tightened, a muscle ticking near his ear. He stood up abruptly, pacing the length of the room, his breath coming in short, ragged bursts. Every memory of that night, every sharp word exchanged, surged forward, a physical pressure building behind his ribs. He slammed his fist onto the wooden surface, the resulting thud echoing the violent upheaval inside him. He couldn't bear to read it; he just needed the confrontation, the acknowledgment of the wrong, to finally dissipate.

[story 2]

Elara stared at the pristine white stationery. The sheer audacity of it—years of silence, then this formal acknowledgment. Her hands trembled, not from nervousness, but from a deep, internal tremor that felt like shaking earth. She reread the carefully chosen sentences, each word a deliberate barb. A low, guttural sound escaped her throat, a sound she immediately clamped down on, forcing it back into a tight, controlled silence. She walked to the window, staring out at the indifferent city lights, feeling a hot, prickling sensation crawl up her neck. Her breath hitched. She clenched her fists until her nails dug crescent moons into her palms. This was an insult, a profound disrespect for the damage done. She turned back to the desk, her eyes blazing with a cold, hard intensity, unwilling to let the quiet composure she was desperately trying to maintain crack.

[story 3]

Mark picked up the letter with two fingers, treating the paper as if it were contaminated. He didn't look at the signature; he focused only on the texture of the paper, the slight unevenness of the fold. A deep, internal churning began in his stomach, a nauseating lurch. He dropped the letter onto the floor, the sound muffled by the rug. He began to pace again, faster this time, his steps heavy and deliberate, each footfall a measure of mounting tension. His breathing became shallow, rapid. He ran a hand over his face, feeling the heat radiating off his skin. He wanted to shatter something, to make the reality of the past moment shatter along with this paper. His voice, when he finally spoke, was low, dangerously steady. "You think that fixes anything?"
